summary: Atwood expresses frustration at mysterious zero-byte IO.SYS and MSDOS.SYS files that keep reappearing in his Windows root directory despite repeated deletion. These legacy DOS files serve as unwanted reminders of older operating systems he no longer runs. The brief post captures the annoyance of dealing with vestigial system artifacts that refuse to stay deleted.
key_points: Zero-byte IO.SYS and MSDOS.SYS files mysteriously reappear in the root directory after deletion
These files are legacy artifacts from DOS/Win9x era that shouldn't exist on modern Windows
The persistence of these phantom files triggers mild OCD-like frustration
money_quotes: No matter how many times I delete these spurious DOS-related files, the next time I look – they're back
It's a gentle reminder of the Bad Old Days
What's next? AUTOEXEC.BAT and CONFIG.SYS? HIMEM.SYS and optimizing my high memory area? Argh.
key_insight: Legacy system artifacts have a way of haunting modern operating systems, serving as persistent reminders of computing's messy evolutionary history.
